content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 6, 1991


Charles A. Szychowski
4215 Mobley Avenue
La Sierra, CA 92505

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-91-340

Dear Mr. Szychowski:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Your request is general in nature and seeks general guidance with respect to various provisions of the Act. Accordingly, we provide you with informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18329.2

QUESTIONS

We understand your questions to be as follows:

1. Under the provisions of the Act, what is the distinction between a candidate controlled committee and a non-controlled committee primarily formed to support a candidate?

2. Under the provisions of the Act, is it permissible to collect campaign contributions and make expenditures on behalf of an individual who has not declared his or her candidacy and who has not filed a Commission Form 501?

CONCLUSIONS

1. In broad terms, a candidate controlled committee is a committee which is controlled directly or indirectly by a

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. 91-340
Page No. 2

candidate or which acts jointly with a candidate or controlled
committee in connection with the making of expenditures. A non-
controlled committee primarily formed to support a candidate is a
committee which receives contributions and makes expenditures to
support a specific candidate but which is not controlled by the
candidate.

2. Under the provisions of the Act, an individual may
qualify as a candidate even when he or she has not filed a
declaration of candidacy. However, a candidate may not solicit or
accept any contributions until he or she has filed a statement of
intention to be a candidate (Form 501).

ANALYSIS

As we understand your question, you are requesting our advice
to distinguish between a candidate controlled committee and a non-
controlled committee primarily formed to support a candidate. You
also wish to ascertain whether it is permissible to collect
campaign contributions and make expenditures on behalf of an
individual who has not declared his or her candidacy and who has
not filed a Commission Form 501. In order to clarify these
issues, we shall analyze the pertinent definitions contained in
the Act to distinguish the terms "controlled committee,"
"primarily formed committee," and "independent expenditure."

Contribution

With some exceptions, the Act defines a contribution as
follows:

"Contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness
of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or
an enforceable promise to make a payment except to
the extent that full and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes....

Section 82015.

Generally, a payment received by a candidate is a contribution unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it
was received for personal purposes unrelated to his or her
candidacy or status as an officeholder. (Regulation 18215, Evans
Advice Letter, No. I-91-031.)

Committee

The Act requires that all persons or organizations which
receive contributions totaling $1,000 in a calendar year for

Our File No. 91-340
Page No. 3

political purposes to register and file periodic reports disclosing contributions received and expenditures made. (Sections
84101, 84200-84226.) "Committee" means any person or combination
of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year.

(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year; or

(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or committees.

A person or combination of persons that
becomes a committee shall retain its status as a
committee until such time as that status is
terminated pursuant to Section § 84214.

Section 82013.

"Contributions" and "expenditures" are defined in part as
payments made for political purposes. (Sections 82011 and 82025.)

Within ten days of receiving contributions totaling $1,000 or
more, a committee must file a Statement of Organization (Form 410)
with the Secretary of State. (Section 84101.) On the Statement
of Organization, the committee must disclose the "full name,
street address and telephone number, if any, of the treasurer and
other principal officers." (Section 84102(c).) In addition, Section 84103(a) requires that whenever there is a change in any of
the information contained in a statement of organization, an
amendment must be filed within ten days to reflect the change.
Committees are required to file periodic statements disclosing
contributions received and expenditures made. (Sections 84200-
84204.)

Controlled Committee

A controlled committee is one which is controlled directly or
indirectly by a candidate or which acts jointly with a candidate
in connection with the making of expenditures. (Section 82016.)
A candidate controls a committee if the candidate, the candidate's

Our File No. 91-340
Page No. 4

agent, or any other committee controlled by the candidate has a
significant influence on the actions or decisions of the
committee. (Section 82016.)

The Commission has interpreted the definition of "controlled
committee" very broadly to include any significant participation
by a candidate, his or her agent, or representatives of any other
committee he or she controls in the actions of a committee. To
determine whether a candidate controls a committee, we look at the
degree of the candidate's involvement in the committee's
activities. The involvement of a candidate includes the
involvement of his or her campaign committee and his or her
agents. (Davis Advice Letter, No. I-90-173.)

Non-Controlled Committee Primarily Formed to Support a Candidate

A non-controlled "primarily formed committee" is a committee
pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 82013 which is formed or
exists primarily to support or oppose a single candidate or a
single measure, or a group of specific candidates or a group of
specific measures being voted upon in the same city or county
election. (Section 82047.5.) However, the candidate or
candidates which the committee supports must not exercise any
control over the activities of the committee.

A committee which is not a controlled committee may make
independent expenditures on behalf of a candidate. "Independent
expenditure" means an expenditure made in connection with a
communication which expressly advocates the election of a clearly
identified candidate but which is not made to or at the behest of
the affected candidate or committee. (Section 82031.)

Regulation 18215(b) provides that an expenditure is made at
the behest of a candidate if it is "made under the control of, at
the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or
concert with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate."
Whether a committee makes independent expenditures or
contributions will depend on the specific facts of the situation.
(Mountjoy Advice Letter, No. I-89-426; Craigie Advice Letter, No.
A-89-236.)

Accordingly, if you intend to raise funds, make contributions
to, or make independent expenditures to support a candidate, you
will incur filing obligations as a committee. All payments that
the committee makes must be reported on the committee's regular
semi-annual or pre-election campaign statements. (Section 84211.)

Our File No. 91-340
Page No. 5


Campaign funds, whether received by a candidate or a
committee primarily formed to support a candidate, are subject to
the statutory restrictions governing the use of campaign funds.
(Sections 89510-89521.)

The general rule is that any expenditure of campaign funds
must be, at a minimum, reasonably related to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.)
Where an expenditure confers a substantial personal benefit on the
candidate or other controlling person, the expenditure must be
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.³

All receipts and expenditures of campaign funds are subject
to the statutory provisions which impose disclosure and filing
obligations.

In contrast, when an individual collects money and makes
payments for the benefit of a private citizen who is not a
candidate and who does not intend to run for office, the payment
may be in the nature of a personal gift rather than a contribution
or an independent expenditure under the provisions of the Act.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.⁴

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Bianca M. Breeze
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BMB:dg


___________________________
³ A "substantial personal benefit" is defined as a direct
personal benefit to the candidate of $100 or more. (Section
89511(b)(2); Regulation 18580.)

⁴ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

We understand your questions to be as follows:

1. Under the provisions of the Act, what is the distinction between a candidate controlled committee and a non-controlled committee primarily formed to support a candidate?

2. Under the provisions of the Act, is it permissible to collect campaign contributions and make expenditures on behalf of an individual who has not declared his or her candidacy and who has not filed a Commission Form 501?

candidate or which acts jointly with a candidate or controlled committee in connection with the making of expenditures. sections.analysis:
As we understand your question, you are requesting our advice to distinguish between a candidate controlled committee and a non-controlled committee primarily formed to support a candidate. You also wish to ascertain whether it is permissible to collect campaign contributions and make expenditures on behalf of an individual who has not declared his or her candidacy and who has not filed a Commission Form 501. In order to clarify these issues, we shall analyze the pertinent definitions contained in the Act to distinguish the terms "controlled committee," "primarily formed committee," and "independent expenditure."

Contribution

With some exceptions, the Act defines a contribution as follows:

"Contribution" means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes....

Section 82015.

Generally, a payment received by a candidate is a contribution unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it was received for personal purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as an officeholder. (Regulation 18215, Evans Advice Letter, No. I-91-031.)

Committee

The Act requires that all persons or organizations which receive contributions totaling $1,000 in a calendar year for

political purposes to register and file periodic reports disclosing contributions received and expenditures made. (Sections 84101, 84200-84226.) "Committee" means any person or combination of persons who directly or indirectly does any of the following:

(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year.

(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees.

A person or combination of persons that becomes a committee shall retain its status as a committee until such time as that status is terminated pursuant to Section § 84214.

Section 82013.

"Contributions" and "expenditures" are defined in part as payments made for political purposes. (Sections 82011 and 82025.)

Within ten days of receiving contributions totaling $1,000 or more, a committee must file a Statement of Organization (Form 410) with the Secretary of State. (Section 84101.) On the Statement of Organization, the committee must disclose the "full name, street address and telephone number, if any, of the treasurer and other principal officers." (Section 84102(c).) In addition, Section 84103(a) requires that whenever there is a change in any of the information contained in a statement of organization, an amendment must be filed within ten days to reflect the change. Committees are required to file periodic statements disclosing contributions received and expenditures made. (Sections 84200-84204.)

Controlled Committee

A controlled committee is one which is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate or which acts jointly with a candidate in connection with the making of expenditures. (Section 82016.) A candidate controls a committee if the candidate, the candidate's

agent, or any other committee controlled by the candidate has a significant influence on the actions or decisions of the committee. (Section 82016.)

The Commission has interpreted the definition of "controlled committee" very broadly to include any significant participation by a candidate, his or her agent, or representatives of any other committee he or she controls in the actions of a committee. To determine whether a candidate controls a committee, we look at the degree of the candidate's involvement in the committee's activities. The involvement of a candidate includes the involvement of his or her campaign committee and his or her agents. (Davis Advice Letter, No. I-90-173.)

Non-Controlled Committee Primarily Formed to Support a Candidate

A non-controlled "primarily formed committee" is a committee pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 82013 which is formed or exists primarily to support or oppose a single candidate or a single measure, or a group of specific candidates or a group of specific measures being voted upon in the same city or county election. (Section 82047.5.) However, the candidate or candidates which the committee supports must not exercise any control over the activities of the committee.

A committee which is not a controlled committee may make independent expenditures on behalf of a candidate. "Independent expenditure" means an expenditure made in connection with a communication which expressly advocates the election of a clearly identified candidate but which is not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate or committee. (Section 82031.)

Regulation 18215(b) provides that an expenditure is made at the behest of a candidate if it is "made under the control of, at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of a candidate." Whether a committee makes independent expenditures or contributions will depend on the specific facts of the situation. {Mountjoy Advice Letter, No. I-89-426; Craigie Advice Letter, No. A-89-236.)

Accordingly, if you intend to raise funds, make contributions to, or make independent expenditures to support a candidate, you will incur filing obligations as a committee. All payments that the committee makes must be reported on the committee's regular semi-annual or pre-election campaign statements. (Section 84211.)

Campaign funds, whether received by a candidate or a committee primarily formed to support a candidate, are subject to the statutory restrictions governing the use of campaign funds. (Sections 89510-89521.)

The general rule is that any expenditure of campaign funds must be, at a minimum, reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.) However, where an expenditure confers a substantial personal benefit on the candidate or other controlling person, the expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.3

All receipts and expenditures of campaign funds are subject to the statutory provisions which impose disclosure and filing obligations.

In contrast, when an individual collects money and makes payments for the benefit of a private citizen who is not a candidate and who does not intend to run for office, the payment may be in the nature of a personal gift rather than a contribution or an independent expenditure under the provisions of the Act.

We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry. Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.4
